what_it_does: Reframes a sync-architecture decision from a coarse one-way/two-way choice into three independent axes and states the evaluation criteria that will be used to score each option.
observations: The file defines three orthogonal axes: A · Write direction, B · Reconciliation authority, C · Storage topology.
Each axis has two named options, producing a product-space of candidate architectures.
The evaluation criteria are identity-law intactness, two-writer conflict behavior, and iOS write-back possibility.
It explicitly notes that the iOS app + sync requirement makes pure read-only an insufficient answer.
It references scoring in §5, which is not contained in this file.
It links to item-80c37615 as part_of.
standouts: The three-axis decomposition of sync architecture.
The iOS write-back requirement treated as a forcing constraint rather than an optional feature.
conventions: YAML frontmatter with id, address, type, source, state, links, and history.
Markdown section heading and table for the axes.
Uses board:// opaque addresses and § notation for cross-section references.
concerns: The claimed orthogonality of the three axes is asserted but not demonstrated in this file.
The §5 scoring table is referenced but not included, so this file is not self-contained.
notes: This is a design-decision framing artifact that breaks the sync problem into three axes and sets the rubric for comparing architectures.
questions: How is orthogonality between the three axes verified in implementation?
What are the exact mechanical differences between 'vault over the canonical dir' and 'derived parallel vault'?
How does the runtime enforce reconciliation authority versus last-writer-wins?
What does 'edges = our address array' mean for identity law?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to separate the decision into three independent axes, which matches the content.
apparent_themes: Preservation of identity law / opaque addressing
iOS write-back as a hard constraint
intention_signals: It appears to be trying to replace an oversimplified one-way/two-way sync debate with a structured decision space and a shared, file-local evaluation rubric.
novelty: The three-axis product-space model for sync architecture, combined with an explicit identity-law preservation criterion.
contribution: Provides a reusable decision-space decomposition and evaluation rubric that a fused system could keep as a design-reasoning artifact.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard item that decomposes a sync-architecture decision into three orthogonal axes and defines three evaluation criteria for scoring candidate architectures.
intention_for_embedding: To establish a structured way to compare sync architectures while preserving an opaque address identity law and accommodating iOS write-back.
